questions.stem: The apparatus shown is used to test a property of compound R. A lamp is in series with a cell and two electrodes dipping into a beaker. The lamp does not light when the beaker contains pure water. When compound R is dissolved in the water, the lamp lights. Which row describes compound R?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: covalent bonding; compound R made of a metal and a non-metal

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: covalent bonding; compound R made of non-metals only

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: ionic bonding; compound R made of non-metals only

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: ionic bonding; compound R made of a metal and a non-metal

parts.model_answer_html:
D - ionic bonding; metal + non-metal.

The lamp lights only when the circuit is complete through the liquid - that means charged particles (ions) must be moving. A liquid that lets electricity through this way is an electrolyte. Pure water alone is a non-electrolyte (almost no free ions), so the dissolved compound R must be supplying the ions.

Solutions that are electrolytes come from ionic compounds, which form between a metal (loses electrons -> positive ion) and a non-metal (gains electrons -> negative ion). Covalent compounds (e.g. ethanol, sugar) do not conduct in solution. So R has ionic bonding and is made of a metal + non-metal -> D.

questions.stem: Which row describes the extraction of iron from its ore? (name of ore / method of extraction)

parts.prompt: Pick the correct ore and extraction method for iron.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: bauxite / reduction in a blast furnace

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: hematite / reduction in a blast furnace

parts.model_answer_html:
D - hematite / reduction in a blast furnace.
Iron's ore is hematite (Fe2O3). Bauxite is the ore of aluminium. From the chapter, electrolysis is used industrially for very reactive metals like aluminium, sodium and magnesium. Iron is below carbon in reactivity, so it is reduced cheaply by carbon (coke) in a blast furnace.
chapter_refs: Electrolysis of molten binary ionic compounds (industrial extraction)

questions.stem: Which row shows an electrolyte and the products of electrolysis for this electrolyte?

parts.prompt: Pick the electrolyte and its correct electrolysis products.

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: concentrated aqueous sodium chloride / sodium and chlorine

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: molten potassium bromide / hydrogen and bromine

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: molten sodium chloride / sodium and chlorine

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: dilute sulfuric acid / hydrogen and sulfur dioxide

parts.model_answer_html:
C - molten sodium chloride -> sodium at cathode and chlorine at anode.
Apply the chapter rule for molten binary ionic compounds: metal at cathode, non-metal at anode.

Why the others are wrong:
A - concentrated NaCl(aq) gives hydrogen at the cathode (H+ discharged over Na+), not sodium.
B - molten KBr has no water, so hydrogen cannot form. Correct products would be K and Br2.
D - electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid decomposes water, giving H2 and O2 (not SO2) in a 2:1 ratio.
chapter_refs: Electrolysis of molten binary ionic compounds
Electrolysis of concentrated sodium chloride solution
